nativeRange.habitat: Mainstream rivers and floodplains; fast-flowing streams with rocky, gravel substrate and moderate to strong current

diet.notes: In the wild, the Red Tailed Black Shark is primarily an aufwuchs grazer (an animal that scrapes algae and biofilm from hard surfaces such as rocks). In captivity, it generally accepts sinking pellets, quality flakes, and frozen or live foods such as bloodworm, Daphnia, and Artemia, as well as blanched vegetables like peas, cucumber, and courgette. Despite its grazing habit, it is not a reliable algae-control fish and should not be expected to serve the role that dedicated algae eaters such as Otocinclus or Bristlenose Plecos fill.

goodWith: fast-moving mid-water schooling fish (barbs, danios, larger tetras)
similarly sized robust fish that occupy different tank zones
avoidWith: Rainbow Shark (Epalzeorhynchos frenatum — similar shape triggers severe aggression)
Bala Shark (visual similarity may provoke attacks)
angelfish, bettas, fancy guppies and other slow or long-finned fish

imageCandidates.notes: Astellar87, 3648x2736. Tack-sharp full-body profile: jet-black velvety body, erect shark-like dorsal fin, and vivid blood-red caudal fin — textbook diagnostic look.
Astellar87, 3648x2736. Sharp whole-fish shot, deep black body with bright red forked tail; slight green cast from planted tank but colours clearly diagnostic.
Caveman99, 1024x768. Different individual, full body in frame with black body and orange-red tail; lower resolution and slightly softer, kept as variety.

summary: The Red Tailed Black Shark (Epalzeorhynchos bicolor) is a Thai freshwater cyprinid that is CRITICALLY ENDANGERED in the wild but widely captive-bred. It is intensely territorial and must be kept alone — two individuals will fight to the death. Best suited to a spacious, well-decorated tank of at least 200 litres with robust, fast-moving tankmates.
careNotes:
CONSERVATION: Listed as Critically Endangered on the IUCN Red List. Wild populations were declared extinct in 1996 but small populations were rediscovered in central/western Thailand (Mae Klong, Chao Phraya, Bangpakong river systems) between 2011 and 2014. Virtually all trade specimens are captive-bred, primarily on Thai farms using hormone-induced spawning — wild collection is essentially nonexistent.

TAXONOMY: The species was formerly classified under the genus Labeo as Labeo bicolor before being reclassified to Epalzeorhynchos bicolor. The genus name Epalzeorhynchos is frequently misspelled in trade sources.

AGGRESSION: This species has been documented as one of the most territorial cyprinids in the freshwater hobby. Each individual tends to claim and defend a territory of roughly 1 metre diameter. Conspecific encounters generally escalate to fatal combat — NEVER keep two together under any circumstances. The aggression also extends strongly toward other visually similar species: the Rainbow Shark (Epalzeorhynchos frenatum) and Bala Shark (Balantiocheilos melanopterus) should be avoided as their similar body shape and colouration appear to trigger territorial responses. Bottom-dwelling catfish and loaches may also be harassed, depending on the individual fish.

FIN-NIPPING: Has been known to target long-finned or slow-moving fish, including angelfish, bettas, and fancy guppies. Keep only with robust, fast-moving species.

TANK SETUP: A long footprint (minimum 120 cm / ~4 ft) is generally considered more important than volume alone, as this species patrols a defined bottom territory. Provide multiple caves, rocky overhangs, and driftwood to break sightlines and give other inhabitants refuge. Dense planting or rockwork helps define territories and reduces aggression toward tankmates. Strong water flow (4–5× tank turnover) replicates the species' native river habitat.

SIZE CAUTION: Juveniles are commonly sold at 3–5 cm and appear manageable, but have been known to reach full adult size (12–15 cm) within 12–18 months.
breedingNotes: Not bred in private aquaria. Commercial production relies entirely on hormone-induced spawning at large-scale farms, primarily in Thailand. No reliable external sexing criteria are documented for juveniles; adult females have been reported as slightly larger and fuller-bodied. Hobbyists should not expect to breed this species.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Aquarium Co-Op species-specific page returned 404; SeriouslyFish used as primary source. Provided tank dimensions (120×45 cm base = ~243 L), water parameters, native range, conservation status, temperament, diet, and breeding notes.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Secondary source (general freshwater sharks blog). Confirmed adult size 13–15 cm, pH 6–8, temperature 22–26°C, 4-foot minimum tank length, solitary/territorial temperament. Used as supporting cross-verification.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed max size 13 cm TL, temperature 22–26°C, pH 6.5–7.5, hardness up to 15 dH, native range (Chao Phraya basin, Thailand), IUCN Critically Endangered status, family Cyprinidae, order Cypriniformes.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size ~15 cm (6 in), 55 gal (208 L) minimum tank, temperature 22–26°C, pH 6.0–8.0, hardness 5–15 dGH, lifespan 5–8 years, omnivore diet, conspecific aggression, bottom swimmer.

fish.breedingNotes: Not bred in home aquaria. Commercial production uses hormone-induced spawning on large Thai farms. No reliable external sexing criteria for hobbyists.
